content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
PO BOX 907 • SACRAMENTO, 95814 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

November 28, 1977

Joseph Cucchiara
Post Office Box 128
Brookdale, California 95007

Dear Mr. Cucchiara:

This letter will confirm the conversations that I have had
with you and Mr. Amrhein during the past week. I understand
that there is some confusion in the San Lorenzo District as to
the meaning of the Commission's informal advice. Accordingly,
you have asked two questions.

First, you wish to know why your original request was not
answered in a formal opinion. Second, you wish to know the
effect of informal advice.

With respect to your first question, formal opinions usually
are issued only when the Commission is presented either with a
question of general statewide applicability or a novel question
involving an interpretation of the Act. It was the staff's
and Commission's judgment that the request posed by Mr. Amrhein
did not fall under either such category; rather, it asked a
series of complex questions involving a settled interpretation
of the Act.

With respect to your second question, the informal advice
in this instance was prepared by the staff and reviewed by the
Commission prior to its issuance. The fact that the advice is
labeled "informal", as opposed to "formal", does not mean that
the advice is casual or unreliable. The same effect of such a
classification we noted: The letter will not be published,
and a person who reasonably relies on the advice contained in
the letter technically is immune only from prosecution by the
Commission and not from other citizen or agencies in the state.
However, as a practical matter, a person who relies on the

Joseph Caretti
November 28, 19[illegible]
Page Two

contents of an internal advice letter generally will be protected
from prosecution by any other such agency or person.

I trust that this answer your questions concerning the
issuance of internal advice with regard to Director Peterson
and the A.T. If you have any further questions, please do
not hesitate to contact me.

Sincerely,

Paul Prime
Chief
Conflicts of Interest Division

From

cc: Mr. Roy Ambrosi

Joseph Cucchiara
P.O. Box 128
Brookdale, Ca. 95007
408-338-2465


Mr. Ted Prim
Conflict of Interest Division
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 167
Sacramento, Ca. 95804

Nov. 16, 1977

Dear Mr. Prim:

I am a property owner and water customer of the San Lorenzo
Valley County Water District (SLVCWD). At a special meet-
ing of the water district in which I was present, on Thurs-
day, November 10, 1977, at 11 a.m., Mr. R. W. Johnson,
Director of SLVCWD made reference to Opinion Request No.
77-023 concerning Director Edwin Peterson of the SLVCWD. I
was informed that the FPPC issued a response to that request
on October 7, 1977 in the form of informal advise.

Some confusion has arisen as a result of statements made at
the above meeting by Director Johnson regarding the lack of
validity and insignificance of the informal advice rendered
by the FPPC. Mr. Amrine regarding request on August 20, 1977, con-
cerned Director Edwin Peterson of the SLVCWD, and the dis-
qualification provisions of the Political Reform Act, pur-
suant to Government Code Section 8414(b).

As a concerned citizen of the district I would appreciate
your response to the following questions:

1. Why did the FPPC not accept the request for a
formal response?

2. What is the effect of informal advise as rendered
by the FPPC?

Thank you for your concern and efforts in responding to
my questions.

Sincerely,

Joseph Cucchiara

I understand that there is some confusion in the San Lorenzo District as to the meaning of the Commission's informal advice. Accordingly, you have asked two questions. First, you wish to know why your original request was not answered in a formal opinion. Second, you wish to know the effect of informal advice. With respect to your first question, formal opinions usually are issued only when the Commission is presented either with a question of general statewide applicability or a novel question involving an interpretation of the Act. It was the staff's and Commission's judgment that the request posed by Mr. Amrhein did not fall under either such category; rather, it asked a series of complex questions involving a settled interpretation of the Act. With respect to your second question, the informal advice in this instance was prepared by the staff and reviewed by the Commission prior to its issuance. The fact that the advice is labeled "informal", as opposed to "formal", does not mean that the advice is casual or unreliable. The main effects of such a classification are twofold: The letter will not be published, and a person who reasonably relies on the advice contained in the letter technically is immune only from prosecution by the Commission and not from other citizens or agencies in the state. However, as a practical matter, a person who relies on the The contents of an informal advice letter generally will be protected from prosecution by any other such agency or person. I trust that this answers your questions concerning the issuance of informal advice with regard to Director Peterson under the Act. If you have any further questions, please do not hesitate to contact me. Sincerely, Ted Price Chief Conflicts of Interest Division cc: Mr. Roy Amrhein Joseph Cucchiara P.O.
